The **2018 Asian Games** ([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"): *Pesta Olahraga Asia 2018* or *Asian Games 2018*), officially known as the **18th Asian Games** ([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"): *Pesta Olahraga Asia ke-18*), and also known as **Jakarta-Palembang 2018** or **Indonesia 2018**, were a [continental](/wiki/Asia "Asia") [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") that was held from 18 August to 2 September 2018 in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") and [Palembang](/wiki/Palembang "Palembang").

For the first time, the Summer [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games") were co-hosted by two regions; the Indonesian capital of Jakarta (which was hosting the Games for the first time since [1962](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games")), and Palembang, the capital of [South Sumatra](/wiki/South_Sumatra "South Sumatra") province. Events were held in and around the two cities, including venues in [Bandung](/wiki/Bandung_Regency "Bandung Regency") and the provinces of [West Java](/wiki/West_Java "West Java") and [Banten](/wiki/Banten "Banten"). The [opening](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games_opening_ceremony "2018 Asian Games opening ceremony") and [closing](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games_closing_ceremony "2018 Asian Games closing ceremony") ceremonies of the Games were held at [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") in Jakarta. The Games were originally awarded to [Hanoi, Vietnam](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi"), but withdrew in 2014 due to budgetary concerns and other factors.

Several non-Olympic events were trimmed from the event programme, but several new disciplines being introduced at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") (including 3-on-3 basketball) were added. [Esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") and [canoe polo](/wiki/Canoe_polo "Canoe polo") were also contested as [demonstration sports](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport").

China led the medal tally for the tenth consecutive time. [North](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") fielded a [unified team](/wiki/Korea_at_the_2018_Asian_Games "Korea at the 2018 Asian Games") during the opening ceremony and selected events, and also won their first-ever gold medal as a unified team at a multi-sport event. 6 world, 18 Asian and 86 Asian Games records were broken during the Games, while Japanese swimmer [Rikako Ikee](/wiki/Rikako_Ikee "Rikako Ikee") was announced as the [most valuable player](/wiki/Most_valuable_player "Most valuable player").[[3]](#cite_note-465events-4)

## Bidding process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Bidding process")]

Main article: [Bids for the 2018 Asian Games](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2018_Asian_Games "Bids for the 2018 Asian Games")

The OCA originally planned to hold these Games in 2019 rather than 2018, so that Asian Games would be held exactly a year before the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") instead of the same year as the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics"). After they were awarded to Indonesia, the OCA backtracked on these plans and kept the Games in 2018, so that they would not interfere with the [2019 Indonesian general election](/wiki/2019_Indonesian_general_election "2019 Indonesian general election").[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)

### Initial bidding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Initial bidding")]

[Hanoi](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi"), Vietnam was originally selected to be the host after they won the bid against two other candidates, [Surabaya](/wiki/Surabaya "Surabaya") and [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"). They were awarded the winning bid on 8 November 2012, with 29 votes against Surabaya's 14 votes.[[8]](#cite_note-Indonesia-9) Dubai pulled out at the last minute, instead announcing their intention to focus on future bids. The UAE's National Olympic Committee's vice-president denied any pullout and claimed that Dubai "did not apply for hosting 2019 Asian Games" and had "only considered" doing so.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-cost-12)

However, in March 2014, there were some concerns about Vietnam's ability to host. These included concerns over whether the anticipated budget of US$150 million was realistic. There were claims that the government would eventually spend over US$300 million. In addition, critics were concerned that several stadiums built in conjunction with the [2003 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2003_Southeast_Asian_Games "2003 Southeast Asian Games") had not been utilized since.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Former chairman of the Vietnam Olympic Committee Ha Quang Du also claimed that hosting the Asian Games would not boost tourism in Vietnam.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

On 17 April 2014, Vietnamese Prime Minister [Nguyễn Tấn Dũng](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_T%E1%BA%A5n_D%C5%A9ng "Nguyễn Tấn Dũng") officially announced Hanoi's withdrawal from hosting, citing unpreparedness and economic recession as the main reasons for the withdrawal, saying "they have left the country unable to afford the construction of facilities and venues".[[14]](#cite_note-withdrew-15) Many Vietnamese people supported the decision to withdraw.[[15]](#cite_note-16) No penalty was imposed for the withdrawal.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

### Appointment of Jakarta and Palembang

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Appointment of Jakarta and Palembang")]

After Hanoi's withdrawal, the OCA said that Indonesia, China, and the United Arab Emirates were major candidates under consideration to host. Indonesia was widely regarded as a favourite, since Surabaya was the runner-up of the previous bid,[[17]](#cite_note-18) and willing to do so if selected.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) The Philippines and India expressed their interest about hosting the Games, but India failed to submit a late bid because it was unable to get an audience with Prime Minister [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi") after being given an extended deadline by the OCA.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

On 5 May 2014, the OCA visited several Indonesian cities, including Jakarta, Surabaya, [Bandung](/wiki/Bandung "Bandung"), and Palembang. At this time Surabaya decided to drop their bid to host the Games and instead focus on hosting the already scheduled [2021 Asian Youth Games](/wiki/2021_Asian_Youth_Games "2021 Asian Youth Games").[[22]](#cite_note-23) On 25 July 2014, during a meeting in [Kuwait City](/wiki/Kuwait_City "Kuwait City"), the OCA appointed Jakarta as the host of the Games with Palembang as the supporting host. As the capital and the third biggest city on southern hemisphere, Jakarta was chosen because of its well-equipped sports facilities, adequate transportation networks, and other facilities such as hotels and lodging for guests.[[23]](#cite_note-24) On 20 September 2014, Indonesia signed the host city contract,[[24]](#cite_note-25) and was ceremonially appointed host during the closing ceremony of the [2014 Asian Games](/wiki/2014_Asian_Games "2014 Asian Games") in [Incheon](/wiki/Incheon "Incheon"). An organising committee was formed soon after the appointment.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28)

## Development and preparations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development and preparations")]

### Costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Costs")]

By 2015, the central government had allocated a budget of [IDR](/wiki/Indonesian_rupiah "Indonesian rupiah") 3 trillion (US$224 million) to prepare for the Games, with regional administrations also expected to supply some part of the funding.[[28]](#cite_note-facility-29) By July 2018, the budget allocation for the Games had been reported to be IDR 6.6 trillion (US$450 million) including IDR 869 billion (US$59 million) from sponsorships.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) However, on 2 September 2018, the [Finance Minister of Indonesia](/wiki/Minister_of_Finance_(Indonesia) "Minister of Finance (Indonesia)") [Sri Mulyani](/wiki/Sri_Mulyani "Sri Mulyani") disclosed that IDR 8.2 trillion was financed by 2015–2018 state budget, which was used by the local organizing committee, namely the Indonesia Asian Games Organizing Committee (INASGOC) led by [Erick Thohir](/wiki/Erick_Thohir "Erick Thohir"), for all preparations, opening, organising, and finalizing the implementation of the 2018 Asian Games.[[32]](#cite_note-33) The total cost of arranging the Games is estimated about US$3.2 billion, of which $2.4 billion being spent on infrastructure development associated with the games.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35)

### Volunteers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Volunteers")]

Phase 2 volunteering programme of Jakarta-Palembang 2018 Asian Games began on 18 December 2017 with target of 13,000 volunteers being set.[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) The volunteers wore clothing which included jacket, polo shirts and trousers which they collected from the Uniform Distribution and Accreditation Centre at the Sports and Recreation Arena in East Jakarta. Volunteers also wore accreditation card which gain them access to specific venues and buildings around the site.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### Torch relay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Torch relay")]

Main article: [2018 Asian Games torch relay](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games_torch_relay "2018 Asian Games torch relay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Susi_Susanti.jpg/250px-Susi_Susanti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Susi_Susanti.jpg)

Indonesian badminton legend [Susi Susanti](/wiki/Susi_Susanti "Susi Susanti") carried a torch fire during the Asian Games Torch Relay Concert in the [Special Region of Yogyakarta](/wiki/Special_Region_of_Yogyakarta "Special Region of Yogyakarta"), Indonesia

On 10 May 2018, 100 days before their opening, the Asian Games torch was unveiled. The design was inspired by the [golok](/wiki/Golok "Golok") and skin — traditional weapons originating from Jakarta and Palembang.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

The torch relay began at the [Major Dhyan Chand National Stadium](/wiki/Dhyan_Chand_National_Stadium "Dhyan Chand National Stadium") in [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), host of the [1st Asian Games](/wiki/1951_Asian_Games "1951 Asian Games"), on 15 July 2018. The flame was generated from a parabolic mirror directed at the sun.[[40]](#cite_note-:0-41) On 18 July 2018, a ceremony took place in Brahma field by the 9th century [Hindu temple of Prambanan](/wiki/Prambanan "Prambanan") near [Yogyakarta](/wiki/Yogyakarta "Yogyakarta"), where the torch's flame from India were fused together with an Indonesian natural [eternal flame](/wiki/Eternal_flame "Eternal flame") taken from [Mrapen](/wiki/Mrapen "Mrapen"), [Central Java](/wiki/Central_Java "Central Java"). Subsequently, the Torch Relay Concert were performed marking the start of torch relay throughout the country.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

The relay travelled through 54 cities in 18 provinces in Indonesia, including host cities. The relay covered a total distance of 18,000 kilometres (11,000 mi).[[42]](#cite_note-43) The relay finished on 17 August, the 73rd anniversary of the [Proclamation of Indonesian Independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence"), at the National Monument in Jakarta before being carried into the opening ceremony at [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") the next day.[[40]](#cite_note-:0-41)

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Marketing")]

#### Emblem and mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Emblem and mascot")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/55/2018_Asian_Games_Mascot.svg/250px-2018_Asian_Games_Mascot.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2018_Asian_Games_Mascot.svg)

Bhin Bhin, Kaka, and Atung, the mascots of the 2018 Asian Games

The initial emblem for the 2018 Asian Games was first unveiled on 9 September 2015, in celebration of the country's National Sports Day.[[43]](#cite_note-44) On 27 December 2015, the Games' mascot, Drawa, was unveiled by vice president [Jusuf Kalla](/wiki/Jusuf_Kalla "Jusuf Kalla"). Both the emblem and mascot were a stylized rendition of the *[cenderawasih](/wiki/Paradisaea "Paradisaea")*, a rare species of bird in Indonesia.[[44]](#cite_note-:3-45)[[45]](#cite_note-:4-46)

The designs were widely criticised for their outdated appearance, and Drawa in particular was criticised for having little connection to Indonesian culture and history (with some Indonesians joking that the mascot looked more like a [chicken](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken") than a *cenderawasih*). On 7 January 2016, Kalla announced that the original emblem and mascot had been withdrawn based on feedback, and that an open call for new designs would be held in partnership with the [Creative Economy Agency](/wiki/Ministry_of_Creative_Economy "Ministry of Creative Economy").[[44]](#cite_note-:3-45)[[45]](#cite_note-:4-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Out of 60 submissions, the new emblem — entitled "Energy of Asia" — was unveiled on 28 July 2016. The new emblem was modelled upon the [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium"), and was intended to symbolise unity among Asian countries.[[47]](#cite_note-logomascot-48)[[48]](#cite_note-JP-AG-2018-Logo-Mascot-49) Three new mascots were also unveiled: Bhin Bhin, a [greater bird-of-paradise](/wiki/Greater_bird-of-paradise "Greater bird-of-paradise"); Atung, a [Bawean deer](/wiki/Bawean_deer "Bawean deer"); and Kaka,[[b]](#cite_note-51) a [Javan rhinoceros](/wiki/Javan_rhinoceros "Javan rhinoceros"). They represent the Eastern, Central, and Western regions of Indonesia,[[50]](#cite_note-52) as well as strategy, speed and strength. The mascots' outfits reflect traditional textiles; Bhin Bhin wears a vest with [Asmat](/wiki/Asmat_people "Asmat people") pattern details, Atung wears a *[sarong](/wiki/Sarong "Sarong")* with *[batik tumpal](/wiki/Batik "Batik")* patterns, and Kaka wears a [Palembang](/wiki/Palembang "Palembang") *[songket](/wiki/Songket "Songket")* with floral patterns. Their names were derived from the national motto of Indonesia, *[Bhinneka Tunggal Ika](/wiki/Bhinneka_Tunggal_Ika "Bhinneka Tunggal Ika")* (*Unity in Diversity*).[[51]](#cite_note-53)

#### Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Medals")]

The medal designs were unveiled in July 2018, featuring [batik](/wiki/Batik "Batik") designs reflecting the unity and diversity of Indonesian and Asian culture.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

#### Promotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Promotion")]

On 18 August 2017, simultaneous events were held at Jakarta's [National Monument](/wiki/National_Monument_(Indonesia) "National Monument (Indonesia)") and Palembang's [Kuto Besak Fort](/wiki/Kuto_Besak "Kuto Besak") to mark the one-year milestone before the Games. The event in Jakarta was attended by president [Joko Widodo](/wiki/Joko_Widodo "Joko Widodo"), and featured performances by [Taeyeon](/wiki/Taeyeon "Taeyeon") and [Hyoyeon](/wiki/Hyoyeon "Hyoyeon") of [K-pop](/wiki/K-pop "K-pop") group [Girls' Generation](/wiki/Girls%27_Generation "Girls' Generation"). Countdown clocks were unveiled at the [Selamat Datang Monument](/wiki/Selamat_Datang_Monument "Selamat Datang Monument") and in front of [Gelora Sriwijaya Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Sriwijaya_Stadium "Gelora Sriwijaya Stadium").[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56)

Several [fun run](/wiki/Fun_run "Fun run") events were held in Asian countries to promote the Games, beginning with [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), Pakistan in December 2017.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

#### Merchandising

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Merchandising")]

The games' merchandises and licensing products, which included mascot [stuffed toys](/wiki/Stuffed_toy "Stuffed toy") and other items, were sold in stores across Jakarta (including the Super Store at the [Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex")) from the end of 2017 to the end of September 2018.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)

#### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Energy of Asia: Official Album of Asian Games 2018](/wiki/Energy_of_Asia:_Official_Album_of_Asian_Games_2018 "Energy of Asia: Official Album of Asian Games 2018")

The official theme song of the Games, "Meraih Bintang" ("Reach for the Stars"), was performed by [Via Vallen](/wiki/Via_Vallen "Via Vallen"). The song was included in a larger [official album for the Games](/wiki/Energy_of_Asia:_Official_Album_of_Asian_Games_2018 "Energy of Asia: Official Album of Asian Games 2018"), released on 13 July 2018 and featuring various Indonesian musicians.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

As part of an effort to appeal younger spectators, the Indonesian-Japanese idol group [JKT48](/wiki/JKT48 "JKT48") was signed to perform at some of the Games' venues, such as [jet ski](/wiki/Jet_ski_at_the_2018_Asian_Games "Jet ski at the 2018 Asian Games"), [softball](/wiki/Softball_at_the_2018_Asian_Games "Softball at the 2018 Asian Games"), and [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball_at_the_2018_Asian_Games "Volleyball at the 2018 Asian Games").[[60]](#cite_note-62) They performed in select events between 19 August and 1 September in a group consisted of eight members from each of its teams.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

#### Philatelic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Philatelic")]

As part of creating a memorable and collectible item, the Indonesian postal service [Pos Indonesia](/wiki/Pos_Indonesia "Pos Indonesia") issued a philatelic collectible items of the 2018 Asian Games series. These items were issued on 18 January 2018 and 18 August 2018.[[62]](#cite_note-Stamps-64)

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Venues")]

Main article: [Venues of the 2018 Asian Games](/wiki/Venues_of_the_2018_Asian_Games "Venues of the 2018 Asian Games")

The Games used a mix of new and existing venues in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta") and [South Sumatra](/wiki/South_Sumatra "South Sumatra") (with organizers aiming to reuse existing facilities and infrastructure, such as those built for the [2011 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2011_Southeast_Asian_Games "2011 Southeast Asian Games"), to help control costs), as well as Jakarta's neighbouring provinces of [Banten](/wiki/Banten "Banten"), and [West Java](/wiki/West_Java "West Java"). Venues were divided into four clusters, with three in Jakarta and its surrounding areas and one in Palembang.[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[28]](#cite_note-facility-29)

### Jakarta

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Jakarta")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/GBK_Main_Stadium_new_seats.jpg/250px-GBK_Main_Stadium_new_seats.jpg)](/wiki/File:GBK_Main_Stadium_new_seats.jpg)

[Gelora Bung Karno Main Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") hosted the ceremonies and athletics events.

The [Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex") in Jakarta hosted 13 events.[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68) The 56-year-old [Main Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") was refurbished for the Games,[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70) replacing its existing bleachers and seating with an [all-seater](/wiki/All-seater_stadium "All-seater stadium") design (reducing its capacity to 77,193), and adding new sound systems and LED lighting among other enhancements.[[69]](#cite_note-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72)[[71]](#cite_note-73)

The [Jakarta International Velodrome](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Velodrome "Jakarta International Velodrome") at [Rawamangun](/wiki/Rawamangun "Rawamangun") in [East Jakarta](/wiki/East_Jakarta "East Jakarta") was rebuilt, at a cost of US$40 million for cycling, badminton, futsal, basketball, and wrestling.[[72]](#cite_note-74) The [Jakarta International Equestrian Park](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Equestrian_Park "Jakarta International Equestrian Park") at [Pulomas](/wiki/Kelapa_Gading "Kelapa Gading") underwent a US$30.8 million renovation, with a capacity of 1,000, 100 stables, lodging for athletes, and other amenities.[[73]](#cite_note-75)

### Palembang

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Palembang")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Stadion-gelora-sriwijaya-palembang.jpg/250px-Stadion-gelora-sriwijaya-palembang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadion-gelora-sriwijaya-palembang.jpg)

[Gelora Sriwijaya Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Sriwijaya_Stadium "Gelora Sriwijaya Stadium") hosted the final of women's football

[Jakabaring Sport City](/wiki/Jakabaring_Sport_City "Jakabaring Sport City") was used as an event site. While there were preliminary plans to increase the size of [Gelora Sriwijaya Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Sriwijaya_Stadium "Gelora Sriwijaya Stadium") from 36,000 to 60,000 seats, they were shelved. The site still underwent some renovations, including also being converted from bleachers to an all-seater.[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77) A new 40-lane bowling alley was constructed on the complex,[[76]](#cite_note-78) as well as eight additional tennis courts.[[77]](#cite_note-79) The length of the canoeing and rowing venue in Jakabaring Lake was extended to 2,300 meters, while new rowing facilities and a tribune were built on the lake shore.[[78]](#cite_note-80) Other existing venues which was used for Games were also renovated, including Ranau Sports Hall as sepak takraw venue.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

### Athletes' villages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Athletes' villages")]

A 10-hectare [athletes' village](/wiki/Kemayoran_Athletes_Village "Kemayoran Athletes Village") was constructed in Jakarta's [Kemayoran](/wiki/Kemayoran "Kemayoran") district, with 7,424 apartments in 10 towers, and a total capacity of 22,272. A second athletes' village was built at Jakabaring Sports City at Palembang, which housed 3,000 athletes and officials.[[80]](#cite_note-82)[[81]](#cite_note-83)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/LRT_Palembang_Ampera_Station.jpg/250px-LRT_Palembang_Ampera_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:LRT_Palembang_Ampera_Station.jpg)

Ampera LRT Station, one of 13 stations of Palembang LRT

As part of the Games preparation, the construction of the [Jakarta MRT](/wiki/Jakarta_MRT "Jakarta MRT") and [Jakarta LRT](/wiki/Jakarta_LRT "Jakarta LRT") was accelerated, though neither were ready for general commercial operation at the time of the opening ceremonies.[[82]](#cite_note-84) A line of [Jakarta LRT](/wiki/Jakarta_LRT "Jakarta LRT") connected the [Velodrome](/wiki/Velodrome "Velodrome") at [Rawamangun](/wiki/Rawamangun "Rawamangun") in [East Jakarta](/wiki/East_Jakarta "East Jakarta") to the [Kelapa Gading](/wiki/Kelapa_Gading "Kelapa Gading") district of North Jakarta, connecting the Velodrome with the [Equestrian Park](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Equestrian_Park "Jakarta International Equestrian Park") slightly to the north. City bus operator [Transjakarta](/wiki/Transjakarta "Transjakarta") added 416 buses to serve the officials,[[83]](#cite_note-85) and also provide free rides on selected days during the Games.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

Palembang upgraded their transportation facilities ahead for the Games by building 25 kilometres of the [Palembang Light Rail Transit](/wiki/Palembang_Light_Rail_Transit "Palembang Light Rail Transit") from [Sultan Mahmud Badaruddin II International Airport](/wiki/Sultan_Mahmud_Badaruddin_II_International_Airport "Sultan Mahmud Badaruddin II International Airport") to Jakabaring Sports City which opened for public use in July 2018.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88) Initially this LRT line was created and open specifically for the Asian Games 2018 [[87]](#cite_note-89)

Multi-sport event in Jakarta and Palembang, Indonesia

. Sultan Mahmud Badaruddin II International Airport is expanding its existing arrival and departure terminals to increase its capacity and also connecting the airport with the light rail transit (LRT) terminal by building a skybridge.[[88]](#cite_note-90) Other transportation facilities such as [toll roads](/wiki/Trans-Sumatra_Toll_Road "Trans-Sumatra Toll Road"), [flyovers](/wiki/Overpass "Overpass"), and [bridges](/wiki/Bridge "Bridge") will be also built in and around the city.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

## The Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: The Games")]

### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

Main article: [2018 Asian Games opening ceremony](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games_opening_ceremony "2018 Asian Games opening ceremony")

See also: [2018 Asian Games Parade of Nations](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games_Parade_of_Nations "2018 Asian Games Parade of Nations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/2018_Asian_Games_opening_ceremony_14_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-2018_Asian_Games_opening_ceremony_14_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_Asian_Games_opening_ceremony_14_(cropped).jpg)

Opening ceremony

The opening ceremony started at 19:00 [Western Indonesian Time](/wiki/Western_Indonesian_Time "Western Indonesian Time") (UTC+7) on Saturday, 18 August 2018. [Wishnutama](/wiki/Wishnutama "Wishnutama"), then-CEO of Indonesian TV network [NET.](/wiki/NET_(Indonesian_TV_channel) "NET (Indonesian TV channel)") was the creative director for the ceremony.[[90]](#cite_note-92) The ceremony stage showcased a towering 26 meter-high mountain with a waterfall as its background, accompanied by Indonesian plants and flowers.[[91]](#cite_note-:2-93) [North](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") delegates marched together under one [unified flag of Korea](/wiki/Korean_Unification_Flag "Korean Unification Flag"), which marked the first time both countries did so in the Asian Games after 12 years.

The games was officially opened by the [President of Indonesia](/wiki/President_of_Indonesia "President of Indonesia"), [Joko Widodo](/wiki/Joko_Widodo "Joko Widodo").[[91]](#cite_note-:2-93)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Sports")]

In March 2017, the Olympic Council of Asia initially announced that the Games would feature 484 events in 42 sports, including the 28 permanent Olympic sports contested at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics"), the five additional sports that will be contested at the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") in Tokyo, as well as events in other non-Olympic sports.[[92]](#cite_note-94) In April 2017, the OCA approved reductions in the programme in response to cost concerns; belt wrestling, cricket, kurash, skateboarding, sambo, and surfing were dropped from the programme, and there was to be a reduced number of competitions in bridge, jet ski, jujitsu, paragliding, sport climbing, taekwondo (in particular, all non-Olympic weight classes), and wushu. These changes reduced the total number of events to 431.[[93]](#cite_note-95)[[94]](#cite_note-96)

The final programme was unveiled in September 2017, increasing it to 462 events in 40 sports (61 disciplines) as the second-largest programme in Asian Games history.[[3]](#cite_note-465events-4) Additional disciplines being introduced at the 2020 Summer Olympics were also added, including [3x3 basketball](/wiki/3x3_basketball "3x3 basketball") and [BMX freestyle](/wiki/BMX_freestyle "BMX freestyle").[[95]](#cite_note-97)

For the first time in Asian Games history, [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") and [canoe polo](/wiki/Canoe_polo "Canoe polo") were contested as demonstration sports in the Games. Six video game titles, most notably [Pro Evolution Soccer 2018](/wiki/Pro_Evolution_Soccer_2018 "Pro Evolution Soccer 2018"), were featured in the esports events.[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99)

### Participating National Olympic Committees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Participating National Olympic Committees")]

All 45 members of the [Olympic Council of Asia](/wiki/Olympic_Council_of_Asia "Olympic Council of Asia") participated in the games.[[98]](#cite_note-100) North Korea and South Korea competed as a [unified team](/wiki/Unified_Korean_sporting_teams "Unified Korean sporting teams") in some events under the name "Korea" (COR), and marched together under the [Korean Unification Flag](/wiki/Korean_Unification_Flag "Korean Unification Flag") during the opening and closing ceremonies.[[99]](#cite_note-101) With a gold medal in the [Women's 500 metre dragon boat](/wiki/Dragon_boat_at_the_2018_Asian_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_500_metres "Dragon boat at the 2018 Asian Games – Women's 500 metres") competition, a unified team won their first-ever gold medal in a multi-sport event.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

Originally set to compete as Independent Asian Athletes, the Kuwaitis were allowed to compete under their own flags just two days before the opening ceremony.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

### Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

Main article: [2018 Asian Games closing ceremony](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games_closing_ceremony "2018 Asian Games closing ceremony")

The closing ceremony started at 19:00 [Western Indonesia Time](/wiki/Time_in_Indonesia "Time in Indonesia") (UTC+7) on Sunday, 2 September 2018 and ended at 21:25. In addition to local artists and a Chinese segment, the South Korean boybands [Super Junior](/wiki/Super_Junior "Super Junior") and [iKon](/wiki/IKon_(South_Korean_band) "IKon (South Korean band)"), and Indian singer Sidharth Slathia performed in the ceremony.[[136]](#cite_note-138) Mayor of Hangzhou Xu Liyi received the Games flag for the 2022 Games from Jakarta Governor [Anies Baswedan](/wiki/Anies_Baswedan "Anies Baswedan") and South Sumatra Governor [Alex Noerdin](/wiki/Alex_Noerdin "Alex Noerdin").[[137]](#cite_note-139)

## Medal table

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Medal table")]

Main article: [2018 Asian Games medal table](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games_medal_table "2018 Asian Games medal table")

[China](/wiki/China_at_the_2018_Asian_Games "China at the 2018 Asian Games") led the medal table for the tenth consecutive time. [Korea](/wiki/Korea_at_the_2018_Asian_Games "Korea at the 2018 Asian Games") claimed their first gold medal at the Games in the canoeing women's traditional boat race 500 m event.[[138]](#cite_note-140) A total of 37 NOCs won at least one medal, 29 NOCs won at least one gold medal and 9 NOCs failed to win any medal at the Games.

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=). *(September 2023)* |

International Games Broadcast Services (IGBS), a joint venture between Host Broadcast Services (HBS) and IMG Media was appointed by the organiser in July 2017 to serve as the host broadcaster for these Games.[[140]](#cite_note-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143) Involving 37 live production units, it distributed 2,400 hours of the Games content to its international rights holders.[[142]](#cite_note-144)[[143]](#cite_note-145) The International Broadcast Centre was constructed in [Jakarta Convention Center](/wiki/Jakarta_Convention_Center "Jakarta Convention Center"), with a smaller one constructed at the Palembang cluster.[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147)

## Concerns and controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2018_Asian_Games&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Concerns and controversies")]

Main article: [Concerns and controversies at the 2018 Asian Games](/wiki/Concerns_and_controversies_at_the_2018_Asian_Games "Concerns and controversies at the 2018 Asian Games")

Before the Games, authorities in Indonesia were confident both host cities would be ready for the Games although they have had only four years to prepare rather than the usual six after stepping in to fill the gap when Vietnam, whose city of Hanoi was originally chosen to host these Games by the Olympic Council of Asia, dropped out in 2014 citing concerns over costs. On top of that, work in both host cities was delayed throughout 2015 because government funding was not immediately available.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

Various concerns from [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion#Indonesia "Traffic congestion") problems, [series of terror attacks](/wiki/Surabaya_bombings "Surabaya bombings"), which local police claimed is a pre-Asian Games crackdown on terror suspects and petty street criminals,[[147]](#cite_note-:1-149)[[148]](#cite_note-150) and already-provoked Indonesian fans at the [venue](/wiki/Gelora_Joko_Samudro_Stadium "Gelora Joko Samudro Stadium").[[149]](#cite_note-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152)

Jakarta struggled with [air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") and river pollution problems.[[151]](#cite_note-153) River pollution revealed where authorities covered a foul-smelling river near the athletes' village with black nylon mesh over fears it will be an eyesore at the showpiece event.[[152]](#cite_note-154) Governor of Jakarta at that time, and the [Indonesian Environment and Forestry Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Environment_and_Forestry_(Indonesia) "Ministry of Environment and Forestry (Indonesia)") quickly relieved the issues by various solutions.[[153]](#cite_note-155)[[154]](#cite_note-156)

A doping case from a Turkmen wrestler was recorded, which jeopardize the sport's existence for future Games.[[155]](#cite_note-157)
